section.title: The High Priestly Prayer
section.sectionRationale: The Upper Room Discourse closes as Jesus turns from His disciples to His Father in the longest recorded prayer of His life. On the threshold of the cross He prays for His own glorification, for the protection, joy, and sanctification of His disciples, and for the unity and final glory of all who will believe through their message. It is a window into the heart of the Son and His mediating love for His people.
section.thesis: On the eve of the cross Jesus prays that the Father would glorify the Son so the Son may glorify the Father and give eternal life to His own, and He intercedes for His disciples and for all who will believe, that they may be kept, sanctified in the truth, made one as the Father and Son are one, and finally be with Him to see His glory.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Jesus prays for His own glorification and the giving of eternal life; He prays for His disciples, that they be protected, filled with joy, and sanctified in the truth and sent into the world; and He prays for all future believers, that they may be one and at last be with Him to behold His glory.

units.label: Prayer for the Son
units.note: glorify Your Son that He may glorify You

verses.text: When Jesus had spoken these things, He lifted up His eyes to heaven and said, “Father, the hour has come. {{doxazo|Glorify}} Your Son, that Your Son may glorify You.
For You granted Him authority over all people, so that He may give eternal life to all those You have given Him.
Now this is {{zoe|eternal life}}, that they may know You, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom You have sent.
I have glorified You on earth by accomplishing the work You gave Me to do.
And now, Father, glorify Me in Your presence with the glory I had with You before the world existed.

units.label: Prayer for the disciples
units.note: keep them, sanctify them, send them

verses.text: I have revealed Your name to those You have given Me out of the world. They were Yours; You gave them to Me, and they have kept Your word.
Now they know that everything You have given Me comes from You.
For I have given them the words You gave Me, and they have received them. They knew with certainty that I came from You, and they believed that You sent Me.
I ask on their behalf. I do not ask on behalf of the world, but on behalf of those You have given Me; for they are Yours.
All I have is Yours, and all You have is Mine; and in them I have been glorified.
I will no longer be in the world, but they are in the world, and I am coming to You. Holy Father, protect them by Your name, the name You gave Me, so that they may be one as We are one.
While I was with them, I protected and preserved them by Your name, the name You gave Me. Not one of them has been lost, except the son of destruction, so that the Scripture would be fulfilled.
But now I am coming to You; and I am saying these things while I am in the world, so that they may have My joy fulfilled within them.
I have given them Your word and the world has hated them. For they are not of the world, just as I am not of the world.
I am not asking that You take them out of the world, but that You keep them from the evil one.
They are not of the world, just as I am not of the world.
{{hagiazo|Sanctify}} them by the {{aletheia|truth}}; Your word is truth.
As You sent Me into the world, I have also sent them into the world.
For them I sanctify Myself, so that they too may be sanctified by the truth.

units.label: Prayer for all believers
units.note: that they may all be one

verses.text: I am not asking on behalf of them alone, but also on behalf of those who will believe in Me through their message,
that all of them may be one, as You, Father, are in Me, and I am in You. May they also be in Us, so that the world may believe that You sent Me.
I have given them the glory You gave Me, so that they may be one as We are one,
I in them and You in Me, that they may be perfectly united, so that the world may know that You sent Me and have loved them just as You have loved Me.
Father, I want those You have given Me to be with Me where I am, that they may see the glory You gave Me because You loved Me before the foundation of the world.
Righteous Father, although the world has not known You, I know You, and they know that You sent Me.
And I have made Your name known to them and will continue to make it known, so that the love You have for Me may be in them, and I in them.”

doxazo.gloss: To glorify. The prayer opens and returns to glory: the Father glorify the Son so the Son may glorify the Father, and restore to the Son the glory He had before the world existed. The hour of the cross is the hour of mutual glorification, where the Father's love and the Son's obedience shine, and through which the Son gives eternal life.

zoe.gloss: Life, eternal life. Jesus defines it not as mere endless existence but as knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom He sent. Eternal life is relational and present, a knowing of God in Christ that begins now and lasts forever, the very gift the Son was given authority to grant to all the Father gave Him.

hagiazo.gloss: To sanctify, set apart as holy, consecrate. Jesus prays that the Father sanctify the disciples by the truth, and He sanctifies Himself, sets Himself apart to the cross, for their sake. Sanctification here is both being set apart for God's service in the world and being made holy through the truth of God's word.

aletheia.gloss: Truth. Jesus prays, sanctify them by the truth, and adds, Your word is truth. God's word is the means by which His people are set apart and made holy, the reliable revelation that shapes them for God. The same truth is embodied in Christ, so that to be sanctified in the truth is to be conformed to Him.

basic.context: Having finished teaching His disciples, Jesus prays aloud to His Father, and John lets us listen in. First He prays for Himself: the hour has come, so let the Father glorify the Son and the Son glorify the Father, and let the Son give eternal life, which is knowing God. Then He prays for His disciples: that the Father would protect them in a hostile world, fill them with His joy, sanctify them by the truth, and send them out as He was sent. Finally He prays for all who will ever believe through the apostles' message, that they may be one as the Father and Son are one, and that they may be with Him to see His glory.
basic.hermeneutics: This is Jesus interceding, a glimpse into His high priestly heart. The key questions are, what does Jesus most want for His people, and what is eternal life. He wants them kept, made holy, made one, and finally brought to be with Him. Eternal life is defined relationally: knowing the true God and Jesus Christ. Note that Jesus does not ask that His people be removed from the world but kept in it and sent into it, sanctified for mission.
basic.originalLanguages: The prayer circles around key words. Jesus asks the Father to doxazo, glorify, the Son so the Son may glorify the Father. He defines zoe, eternal life, as knowing God and the one He sent. He prays hagiazo, sanctify them, by the aletheia, the truth, which is God's word.
basic.typology: Jesus here fulfills the role of the high priest who intercedes for the people, bearing them on His heart before God, a role the temple priests only foreshadowed. His prayer for unity fulfills the hope of one people of God gathered under one head.
basic.christ: Christ is the eternal Son who shared the Father's glory before the world existed, the one given authority to grant eternal life, and the great High Priest who prays for His people and sanctifies Himself for them. He longs for them to be with Him and to see His glory.
basic.god: God the Father is holy and righteous, the only true God, who loves the Son, gave a people to Him, and loves those people as He loves the Son. The prayer reveals the intimate life of Father and Son into which believers are drawn.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 7 presents Christ interceding as High Priest. John 1 spoke of the Word's glory before creation. Ephesians 4 urges the unity Jesus prays for. 1 John 3 speaks of seeing Him and being like Him.
basic.oneStory: On the edge of the cross, the Son prays as our great High Priest, asking that His people be kept, made holy, made one, and finally brought to be with Him in glory. The prayer gathers the whole purpose of redemption: to draw a people into the love and glory the Father and Son have shared from before the world began.

context.literary: The climactic conclusion of the Upper Room Discourse, a sustained prayer in three concentric movements: for the Son, for the present disciples, and for all future believers. It is bracketed by the language of glory and the hour, and it repeatedly appeals to the Father's giving, sending, and loving. Traditionally called the high priestly prayer, it functions as Jesus' consecration of Himself and His people on the threshold of the Passion.
context.historical: On the night of Passover, as the high priest would intercede for the people, Jesus intercedes as the true High Priest. The theme of the divine name, protect them by Your name, echoes the covenant importance of God's name revealed to Israel. The son of destruction refers to Judas, whose defection fulfills Scripture. The prayer's concern for a persecuted people sent into a hostile world reflects the real situation of the early church.
context.canonical: The prayer anticipates the New Testament's portrait of Christ's ongoing intercession, Hebrews 7:25 and Romans 8:34. Eternal life as knowing God fulfills the covenant promise, Jeremiah 31:34. The prayer for unity grounds the New Testament's call to one body, Ephesians 4:1-6, and the sanctification by the word connects to Ephesians 5:26. The desire that believers be with Him to see His glory anticipates the consummation, 1 John 3:2 and Revelation 22:4.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal the heart of the Son as He prepares for the cross, praying for His own glorification, for the protection, joy, and sanctification of His disciples, and for the unity and final glory of all believers, so that readers grasp what Christ most desires for His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The prayer describes Jesus' intercession on a unique night, but it discloses enduring realities: eternal life is knowing God in Christ, believers are kept and sanctified by the Father, and Christ prays for the unity of all His people. The definition of eternal life and the call to unity and holiness apply to the church in every age.

debates.question: What kind of unity does Jesus pray for in verses 21 to 23?

views.label: Spiritual and relational unity in the truth
views.case: The unity is modeled on the oneness of Father and Son, a living union in love and truth shared by all who are in Christ, not mere institutional merger; it flows from being in Us and sharing the given glory.

views.label: A visible unity that convinces the world
views.case: Jesus ties the unity to the world's believing and knowing, so it must be visible enough to be a witness, a real, observable love and harmony among believers.

debates.bearing: Both belong together: the unity is a genuine spiritual oneness in Christ that becomes visible in love, and its purpose is that the world may believe the Father sent the Son.

debates.question: Why does Jesus say He does not pray for the world in verse 9?

views.label: A focused intercession for His own
views.case: In this specific prayer Jesus concentrates on those the Father has given Him, interceding as their High Priest; it is not a denial of any concern for the world but a particular focus of this prayer.

views.label: Ordered toward the world's believing
views.case: The prayer's aim, that the world may believe and know, shows Jesus' heart still reaches the world; He prays for His people precisely so that through their unity and witness the world may come to faith.

debates.bearing: Together they show that Jesus' focused prayer for His own is not against the world but for it, since the keeping and uniting of His people is the means by which the world is meant to come to faith.

study.originalLanguages: The prayer moves around a few great words. Jesus asks the Father to doxazo, glorify, the Son so that the Son may glorify the Father, and to restore the glory the Son shared before the world existed, for the hour of the cross is the hour of this mutual glory. He defines zoe, eternal life, not as bare endless existence but as knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom He sent, a present and relational life. And for His disciples He prays, hagiazo, sanctify them, set them apart and make them holy, by the aletheia, the truth, adding that God's own word is that truth, the means by which His people are consecrated for their mission in the world.

typology.type: The high priest interceding for the people
typology.fulfillment: Jesus praying as the true High Priest, bearing His people before the Father and consecrating Himself for them, John 17 and Hebrews 7:25.

typology.type: The covenant knowledge of God, Jeremiah 31:34
typology.fulfillment: Eternal life defined as knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom He sent, John 17:3.
The unity of all believers, one as the Father and Son are one, gathered under Christ, John 17:21-23.

study.christ: Christ is revealed as the eternal Son who shared the Father's glory before the world began and now prepares to be glorified through the cross. He is the one given authority over all people to grant eternal life, the knowledge of God, to all the Father has given Him. He is the great High Priest who intercedes for His own, praying for their protection, joy, holiness, and unity, and who sanctifies Himself, sets Himself apart for the cross, on their behalf. His deepest desire is that His people be with Him where He is, to see and share the glory the Father gave Him out of love before the foundation of the world.
study.god: The Father is the only true God, holy and righteous, whose name Jesus reveals and by which He keeps His people. He gave a people to the Son before the world began, loves them as He loves the Son, and draws them into the very communion of love the Father and Son have always shared. The prayer opens a window onto the inner life of God, Father and Son glorifying each other, and shows that the goal of redemption is that this love be in believers and that they behold the Son's glory forever in the presence of the Father who loved them before the foundation of the world.

crossReferences.note: He always lives to intercede for them, the ongoing priestly ministry this prayer foreshadows.
They will all know Me, the covenant knowledge of God that is eternal life in John 17:3.
One body and one Spirit, the unity of believers Jesus prays for.
We shall see Him as He is, fulfilling Jesus' desire that His people see His glory.

study.oneStory: On the threshold of the cross, the Son lifts His eyes to heaven and prays as the great High Priest of His people. He asks that the Father glorify Him through the hour that has come, and that His people be kept in a hostile world, filled with His joy, made holy by the truth, sent out on mission, and knit into one as the Father and Son are one. His final longing is that they be with Him to see His glory and to be filled with the love the Father has had for the Son from before the world began. The whole purpose of redemption is gathered here: to draw a people into the eternal love and glory of God.

quiz.q: How does Jesus define eternal life in this prayer?

options.text: Knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ, whom He sent.
options.note: Verse 3 defines eternal life relationally, as knowing God in Christ.

options.text: Living forever with no more physical death.
options.note: Eternal life includes unending life, but Jesus defines it as knowing God, not merely endless duration.

options.text: Being rewarded for a good and moral life.
options.note: It is not a reward for morality but the gift of knowing God in Christ.

options.text: A restored relationship with God through Christ.
options.note: True and close, but it does not capture the precise definition, knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ.

quiz.q: What does Jesus pray for His disciples regarding the world?

options.text: Not that they be taken out of the world, but that they be kept from the evil one.
options.note: Verse 15 shows Jesus praying for protection in the world, not removal from it.

options.text: That they be removed from the world entirely.
options.note: Jesus explicitly says He is not asking that they be taken out of the world.

options.text: That they be spared all suffering in the world.
options.note: Jesus prays for their protection from the evil one, not that they be spared all hardship or removed from the world.

options.text: That they be protected as they remain in the world.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific request to be kept from the evil one.

quiz.q: How are the disciples to be sanctified, according to Jesus' prayer?

options.text: By the truth, for God's word is truth.
options.note: Verse 17 ties their sanctification to the truth of God's word.

options.text: By their own strenuous efforts to be holy.
options.note: Real holiness follows, but it is God who sanctifies them by the truth of His word, not self-effort alone.

options.text: By withdrawing from all contact with the world.
options.note: They are sanctified in the truth and sent into the world, not withdrawn from it.

options.text: By being set apart through God's word.
options.note: True, but it does not name that the word is truth, the emphasis of the verse.

quiz.q: What does Jesus pray for all who will believe through the apostles' message?

options.text: That they may be one, as the Father and Son are one, so the world may believe.
options.note: Verses 21 to 23 pray for a unity that witnesses to the world.

options.text: That they may be scattered and independent.
options.note: He prays for unity, not division.

options.text: That they may all agree on every minor detail.
options.note: The unity is a deep oneness in Christ, not mere uniformity of opinion.

options.text: That they may be united in love and truth.
options.note: True, but it omits the pattern, one as the Father and Son are one, and the aim, that the world may believe.

quiz.q: What is Jesus' final desire for those the Father has given Him?

options.text: That they be with Him where He is, to see the glory the Father gave Him.
options.note: Verse 24 expresses His longing for His people to be with Him and behold His glory.

options.text: That they be kept safe and blessed in this life.
options.note: He does pray for their keeping, but His final longing is that they be with Him to see His glory, far beyond earthly blessing.

options.text: That they achieve great success in the world.
options.note: His longing is for their presence with Him, not worldly success.

options.text: That they one day share in His presence and glory.
options.note: True, but it softens the specific longing that they be with Him where He is to see His glory.

questions: Jesus defines eternal life as knowing God. How is your relationship with God growing into a deeper knowing, not just knowing about Him?
Jesus prayed for your protection and holiness on the night before He died. How does it move you to know He was thinking of you then?
Jesus did not ask that His people be taken out of the world but kept and sent into it. How are you living as one sent into the world rather than withdrawn from it?
Jesus prayed that believers would be one so the world would believe. What is one relationship where you could pursue that unity for the sake of the gospel?
Jesus longs for you to be with Him and to see His glory. How does that final desire of His shape your hope amid present trials?

translationNotes.note: Authority over all people; the BSB footnote notes the Greek is literally all flesh. The Son is given authority over all humanity to grant eternal life to those the Father gave Him.
Protect them by Your name, the name You gave Me; the BSB footnote notes textual variation. The divine name stands for God's revealed character and covenant presence.
The son of destruction refers to Judas, whose betrayal fulfills Scripture; the phrase echoes the language later used of the man of lawlessness.
Keep them from the evil one; the BSB footnote allows or from evil. Jesus prays for protection from the devil's power rather than removal from the world.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 17:1-26. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. Em dashes joining verses 22 and 23 (one as We are one, I in them and You in Me) were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
